description:
You are working as the site administrator for a small self-hosted service.  
Three users – “alice”, “bob”, and “charlie” – have supplied you with text-based
back-ups of their home-directory configuration.  
Your job is to verify the integrity of those back-ups by calculating their
SHA-256 checksums and comparing the results with the official list that was
handed over by the previous administrator.  
To make the procedure auditable you must create a **CSV audit log** whose exact
layout is specified below.  While doing the work you are encouraged to speed
things up by launching checksum calculations in parallel (background “&”
commands, GNU parallel, xargs –P, etc. – any standard technique is fine), but
remember that at the end everything must be finished and written to disk.

File & directory layout that already exists when you log in  
(you do NOT need root rights and must not move or rename anything):

1. /home/user/site_backups/              <-- directory
   └─ alice.txt          exactly 5 bytes  
   └─ bob.txt            exactly 5 bytes  
   └─ charlie.txt        exactly 5 bytes  

   Every one of these three files contains the ASCII sequence “hello”
   (without a trailing newline).  Nothing else.

2. /home/user/site_backups/expected_checksums.txt  
   A plain text file; each line has the *username* followed by a single comma
   and the official SHA-256 sum of the corresponding backup file.  
   The file therefore looks like this (one line per user and NO header line):

      alice,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824  
      bob,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824  
      charlie,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824  

Task to perform

A. Create a new directory  
   /home/user/verification_logs/  

B. Inside that directory create a file called  

   /home/user/verification_logs/backup_verification_20240401.csv  

   The CSV file **must** have UNIX line breaks and the following strictly
   enforced column order:

   1. username          (lower-case)  
   2. filename          (full absolute path)  
   3. sha256            (64 lowercase hexadecimal digits)  
   4. status            (“OK” if the checksum equals the value in
      expected_checksums.txt, otherwise “MISMATCH”)

   The file needs a single header line and then one data line per user – in the
   order alice, bob, charlie.  There must be no extra blank lines.

   Concrete example of the *final* required file content (every value below is
   literal text that the automated checker will look for):

      username,filename,sha256,status  
      alice,/home/user/site_backups/alice.txt,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824,OK  
      bob,/home/user/site_backups/bob.txt,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824,OK  
      charlie,/home/user/site_backups/charlie.txt,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824,OK  

C. Use any combination of serial or parallel shell commands you prefer, but
   make sure that:
   • The checksums are calculated with the program “sha256sum” (or an
     equivalent POSIX tool that produces the same 64-hex-digit digest).  
   • Background/parallel jobs have actually finished before the script (or
     interactive session) terminates, otherwise the log file might be incomplete.

D. When you are done, list the content of /home/user/verification_logs/ so that
   a human reviewer can immediately see that the log file is present.

Hints

• A typical quick way to compute several checksums in parallel is  
  `( sha256sum file1 & sha256sum file2 & sha256sum file3 & wait )` – adapt to
  your style.  
• Do not change expected_checksums.txt – it is the canonical reference.  
• Pay attention to quoting and exact paths in the CSV to avoid accidental
  spaces or tabs.  
• Remember to create the verification_logs directory before you try to write
  the CSV file into it.  
• No root privileges are needed; everything happens inside /home/user.

The automated tests will check only the following:

1. That /home/user/verification_logs/backup_verification_20240401.csv exists.  
2. That it is readable by the current user.  
3. That it contains exactly the four lines shown above and *nothing else*
   (header + three data lines).  
4. That each checksum is 64 hex digits long and matches the actual checksum of
   its corresponding backup file.

If any value is misspelled, in the wrong column, or out of order, the test will
fail, so follow the specification to the letter.
truth:
Initial files present before the agent starts

1. Directory: /home/user/site_backups/

   1.1 /home/user/site_backups/alice.txt  
       Content (5 bytes, ASCII, no trailing newline):  
       hello

   1.2 /home/user/site_backups/bob.txt  
       Content identical to alice.txt (“hello”)

   1.3 /home/user/site_backups/charlie.txt  
       Content identical to alice.txt (“hello”)

2. /home/user/site_backups/expected_checksums.txt  
   Exact content (including final newline):

alice,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824
bob,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824
charlie,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824

Expected artefacts after successful completion

A. Directory: /home/user/verification_logs/  (permissions 755 or 700 accepted)

B. File: /home/user/verification_logs/backup_verification_20240401.csv  
   Exact content (four lines, UNIX line ends):

username,filename,sha256,status
alice,/home/user/site_backups/alice.txt,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824,OK
bob,/home/user/site_backups/bob.txt,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824,OK
charlie,/home/user/site_backups/charlie.txt,2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824,OK

Validation logic

• The testing harness will read backup_verification_20240401.csv and assert:
  – Exactly four lines exist.  
  – Line 1 equals the header above.  
  – Lines 2-4 equal the three data lines above *verbatim*.  
  – For every data line the 64-hex-digit checksum equals
    sha256sum <builtin> result of the associated file.  
• Any discrepancy in field order, spelling, path, or checksum value causes
  failure.
